output:
Performance Assessment:
  Intermediate-level alto performance in E minor. Pitch accuracy 76% with average error 18 cents (centered); rhythm 95%, breath stability 66%. Overall a controlled performance with clearly localizable weaknesses.

Detected Issues:
  Instability and reduced melodic stability across the register transition. Secondary: Flat intonation and pitch sag, concentrated on sustained and ascending material.

Root Cause Analysis:
  The phrase sits on the Alto passaggio (A3/B3, B4/C5). Without a graded shift in vocal-fold mass and resonance, the voice either flips or braces, producing a break, tonal shift, or pitch wobble right at the transition.
  Insufficient subglottal pressure from weak breath support. As airflow collapses through a phrase, the vocal folds lose the drive needed to hold pitch, so notes settle below target - most visibly on ascending intervals and phrase ends where demand peaks.

Music Theory Explanation:
  The break coincides with movement through iv (subdominant); the harmonic expectation continues while the mechanism destabilizes, so the E minor line loses its melodic through-line exactly where resolution is being set up.

Technique Analysis:
  This is a register-negotiation problem (chest coordination), not an intonation problem per se.

Personalized Coaching:
  Smooth the gearshift: approach the passaggio with a lightening chest coordination and slightly narrowed vowel so the transition is gradual, not abrupt. Given the intermediate level, progress in small, monitored steps.

Recommended Exercises:
  Sirens through the passaggio on [ng]; Octave slides across the break; Lip trills spanning both registers; [i]->[u] five-tone scales through the transition; SOVT straw phonation on sustained pitches

Root Cause Analysis:
  The phrase sits on the Alto passaggio (A3/B3, B4/C5). Without a graded shift in vocal-fold mass and resonance, the voice either flips or braces, producing a break, tonal shift, or pitch wobble right at the transition.

  The break coincides with movement through V7 (dominant (with 7th)); the harmonic expectation continues while the mechanism destabilizes, so the F# minor line loses its melodic through-line exactly where resolution is being set up.

Technique Analysis:
  This is a register-negotiation problem (chest-mix coordination), not an intonation problem per se.

Personalized Coaching:
  Smooth the gearshift: approach the passaggio with a lightening chest coordination and slightly narrowed vowel so the transition is gradual, not abrupt. Given the advanced level, progress in small, monitored steps.

Recommended Exercises:
  Sirens through the passaggio on [ng]; Octave slides across the break; Lip trills spanning both registers; [i]->[u] five-tone scales through the transition

Detected Issues:

Root Cause Analysis:
  Insufficient subglottal pressure from weak breath support. As airflow collapses through a phrase, the vocal folds lose the drive needed to hold pitch, so notes settle below target - most visibly on ascending intervals and phrase ends where demand peaks.
  Timing is destabilized by uneven breath pacing and reactive rather than anticipatory phrasing. When the singer places notes by ear after the beat instead of subdividing internally, tempo sags or rushes across the phrase.

Music Theory Explanation:
  Against V (dominant) in Bb major, sagging pitch weakens the pull toward the A leading tone and blurs resolution to the Bb tonic; the ear reads the phrase as under-supported rather than harmonically arrived.

Technique Analysis:
  Breath-support coordination (appoggio) rather than laryngeal control is the limiting factor here.

Personalized Coaching:
  Rebuild support before pitch: the goal is steady airflow that carries the line without pushing the throat. Anchor the breath low and keep it moving. Given the beginner level, progress in small, monitored steps.

Recommended Exercises:
  SOVT straw phonation on sustained pitches; Messa di voce on comfortable mid-range vowels; Farinelli breathing (4-4-4) for airflow pacing; Sustained [u] with steady appoggio; Metronome subdivision drills

Performance Assessment:
  Beginner-level soprano performance in C# minor. Pitch accuracy 69% with average error 26 cents (sharp); rhythm 53%, breath stability 84%. Overall a partially controlled performance with clearly localizable weaknesses.

Detected Issues:
  Instability and reduced melodic stability across the register transition. Secondary: Rhythmic drift - unstable tempo and inconsistent note placement.

Root Cause Analysis:
  The phrase sits on the Soprano passaggio (E4/F4, E5/F5). Without a graded shift in vocal-fold mass and resonance, the voice either flips or braces, producing a break, tonal shift, or pitch wobble right at the transition.
  Timing is destabilized by uneven breath pacing and reactive rather than anticipatory phrasing. When the singer places notes by ear after the beat instead of subdividing internally, tempo sags or rushes across the phrase.

Music Theory Explanation:
  The break coincides with movement through V7 (dominant (with 7th)); the harmonic expectation continues while the mechanism destabilizes, so the C# minor line loses its melodic through-line exactly where resolution is being set up.

Technique Analysis:
  This is a register-negotiation problem (head-mix coordination), not an intonation problem per se.

Personalized Coaching:
  Smooth the gearshift: approach the passaggio with a lightening chest coordination and slightly narrowed vowel so the transition is gradual, not abrupt. Given the beginner level, progress in small, monitored steps.

Recommended Exercises:
  Sirens through the passaggio on [ng]; Octave slides across the break; Lip trills spanning both registers; [i]->[u] five-tone scales through the transition; Metronome subdivision drills
